html:
When the Mesilla Valley's Monsoon Turns a Dry Roof Into a Claim

Late July through September brings the part of the year that does the most damage to flat commercial roofs around Las Cruces. A monsoon cell can dump an inch of rain in under half an hour, often preceded by a wall of blowing dust that coats drains and scuppers right before the water arrives. That combination, dust-clogged drainage meeting sudden heavy rain, is behind a large share of the monsoon-season claims we document, and it's a different problem than a straightforward wind or hail claim.

Microbursts and Flash Rain on Low-Slope Roofs

A monsoon microburst hits a roof with a short burst of intense wind and rain together, sometimes localized enough that one side of a business park takes real damage while a building three blocks away sees nothing. On flat and low-slope roofs, that combination can pond water faster than drains clear it, especially on older BUR and aggregate-ballast roofs common on downtown and NMSU-adjacent buildings where drainage slopes have settled over decades. We document ponding depth, drain condition, and any membrane displacement from wind-driven water separately, since each points to a different repair need.

Dust-to-Mud Drainage Failures

One pattern specific to this part of New Mexico, a haboob or dust event ahead of a storm cell coats a flat roof's surface and drains with fine desert dust, which turns to mud the moment rain hits it. That mud can partially or fully block a drain within minutes of a storm starting, turning what should have been a manageable rain event into standing water that finds its way through seams and flashing that were otherwise sound. We photograph drain and scupper condition specifically for this reason, since an adjuster needs to see whether the roof's own drainage failed as a contributing factor.

What We Document After a Monsoon Event

  • Dated photos of ponding location, depth, and duration where visible
  • Drain and scupper condition before and after clearing
  • Membrane seam and flashing condition at wind-exposed edges and penetrations
  • Moisture scan results where interior water intrusion has occurred
  • A written scope separating storm-caused damage from pre-existing wear

Roof Systems and How They Read a Monsoon Event

A TPO or PVC single-ply roof generally sheds wind-driven rain well if seams are intact, so monsoon damage on those systems usually shows up at a specific seam failure or penetration rather than across the field. Older BUR and modified bitumen roofs, more common on established buildings near downtown, tend to show damage as isolated blistering or membrane lift where ponding sat longest. Metal roofs on ag-processing and equipment buildings around the valley are less prone to ponding but can take fastener backout or panel lift from the wind gusts that often lead a monsoon cell. We adjust our documentation to whichever system is on the roof.

Timing the Assessment

Monsoon season often brings repeat storms through the same week, which makes fast documentation more important than usual, if a second cell hits before the first event is documented, separating the two in a claim gets harder. We recommend scheduling an assessment within days of any significant monsoon event, even before an interior leak shows up, since surface and drainage damage is easiest to tie to a specific storm date while it's still fresh.

Working the Claim With Your Adjuster

We meet adjusters on the roof and walk through what the drainage and membrane condition shows, in plain terms tied to the specific storm date. We're your roofing contractor documenting and substantiating the damage, not a public adjuster negotiating the claim on your behalf, and we won't promise a particular outcome or inflate a scope to chase a bigger number. What we provide is an accurate record an adjuster can rely on.

Repeat Exposure Across a Full Monsoon Season

Some buildings around the valley take a hit from more than one monsoon cell in a given July-through-September stretch, which means a roof can carry damage from an early-season storm that's still being repaired when a second event arrives. When that happens, we keep the documentation for each storm separate, with its own dated photo set and scope, so an adjuster reviewing a second claim later in the season isn't left trying to untangle which damage belongs to which event. That separation matters most on older aggregate-surfaced roofs, where a second storm can obscure evidence of the first if the two aren't documented independently.

Monsoon Claim Questions From Facilities Managers

Can dust-clogged drains actually cause a covered claim?

The water damage itself can be covered depending on the policy and cause, and documenting that a monsoon event overwhelmed drainage that was otherwise adequate is part of building an accurate claim record.

How fast should we get a roof looked at after a monsoon storm?

As soon as reasonably possible, ideally within days, especially during active monsoon season when a second storm can arrive before the first is documented.

Does ponding water always mean roof damage?

Not necessarily on a roof designed with some drainage lag, but ponding that persists well past a storm or recurs at the same spot usually points to a drainage or slope problem worth documenting.

What if the leak shows up days after the storm?

That's common, water can travel along the deck or insulation before finding a path inside. We can still document the connection to a specific storm date through moisture surveys and membrane inspection.

Do you document damage from multiple monsoon storms separately?

Yes, where a building has taken hits from more than one storm in a season, we document each event's contribution separately so the claim record stays accurate for each.
